behaviors.description: The embedding vector produced for a given query string has the correct dimensionality for the vector database index.
behaviors.justification: To catch real failures like model version changes, API contract changes, or parsing errors, we must call the real embedding model. A mock could hide that the actual API returns unexpected dimensions or response formats. Testing against a deterministic embedding model ensures dimension consistency across all query types.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real embedding model API with a test query string, parse the response, and verify that the returned vector has the correct dimensionality matching the vector database schema (e.g., 1024-dimensional vectors for OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large). Repeat with several queries to ensure consistency.

behaviors.description: The similarity search returns results ordered by descending cosine similarity score.
behaviors.justification: Vector database ordering behavior is implementation-specific (Pinecone, Milvus, Weaviate all have different characteristics). A mock ordering could pass while real database returns unordered or partially-ordered results. A test database instance with controlled data shows whether ordering guarantees are actually honored.
behaviors.test_description: Insert test documents with known embeddings into a test vector database instance, execute searches with test query vectors, and verify that returned results are strictly ordered by descending similarity score. Include edge cases where multiple results have identical or nearly identical scores.

behaviors.description: The LLM re-ranking step changes the order of at least some results compared to the raw similarity ranking when the query has nuanced intent.
behaviors.justification: LLM reranking effectiveness depends on what a real, non-deterministic language model produces. A mocked LLM response (even carefully crafted) cannot validate that the actual model understands nuanced intent or that the prompt engineering elicits appropriate reordering. Real LLM output is required to catch cases where the prompt fails or the model misinterprets intent.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the full search pipeline with a nuanced query (e.g., 'budget-friendly espresso machines under $300' where similarity ranking may not reflect price sensitivity), capture the initial vector-based ranking, send those results to the real LLM with a reranking prompt, and verify that the final ranking differs from the initial ranking in at least some positions.

behaviors.description: A query with no close matches in the database returns an empty result set rather than low-relevance noise.
behaviors.justification: Similarity score ranges and threshold behavior are vector database-specific and depend on real embedding distributions. A mock might use unrealistic score ranges, causing threshold logic to be tested incorrectly. A test database with real data shows whether the filtering threshold logic works correctly with actual similarity distributions.
behaviors.test_description: Run a query against a test vector database with a query far outside the corpus domain (e.g., query about 'nuclear reactor design' in a fashion product database), configure a similarity threshold (e.g., cosine similarity > 0.7), and verify that the API returns an empty result set with zero items, not low-scoring results.

behaviors.description: The end-to-end latency from receiving the query to returning re-ranked results stays below 2 seconds for the median case.
behaviors.justification: Latency measurement requires all real components working together: embedding model (Agentic), vector database (System), and LLM re-ranking (Agentic). Testing embedding and LLM separately or in pairs would miss orchestration overhead, network round-trip costs, and the cumulative delay of the full pipeline. Only a complete workflow test shows whether the end-to-end SLA is met.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the complete search pipeline 100+ times with real embedding model, real vector database, and real LLM re-ranking. Measure total latency from query receipt to final re-ranked result. Calculate p50 latency and verify it stays below 2 seconds. Include realistic network conditions and ensure the LLM inference cost is accounted for.
